class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Business Analytics and Data-Driven Decision Making
learning_objectives: Distinguish between descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics
Identify business problems suited to analytical solutions
Understand the analytics lifecycle: question, data, model, interpret, act
readings: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Business analytics transforms data into insights and insights into action. This course teaches you to apply analytical techniques to real business problems across all functional areas.
key_concepts: Analytics maturity: descriptive (what happened), predictive (what will happen), prescriptive (what should we do)
Analytics lifecycle: problem framing, data collection, analysis, interpretation, action
Types of data: structured, semi-structured, unstructured
Analytics tools landscape: Excel, SQL, Python/R, Tableau, Power BI
Ethics in analytics: bias, privacy, transparency
lecture_notes.demonstration: End-to-end analytics example: using customer data to predict churn and design retention interventions
setup_requirements: Textbook: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics (4th ed.)

review.value: Descriptive summarizes past data; Predictive uses models to forecast; Prescriptive recommends optimal actions
Define business question, gather/clean data, analyze with appropriate methods, interpret results in business context, recommend actions

class_schedule.topic: Data Preparation and Exploratory Data Analysis
learning_objectives: Clean and transform data for analysis
Conduct exploratory data analysis using visualization
Identify data quality issues and handle missing values
readings: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: Data preparation typically consumes 60-80% of an analytics project. The quality of your analysis can never exceed the quality of your data.
key_concepts: Data quality dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness
Missing data handling: listwise deletion, imputation, flagging
Outlier detection and treatment
Feature engineering: creating new variables from existing data
EDA techniques: histograms, scatter plots, correlation matrices, box plots
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cleaning a messy real-world dataset in Python/pandas: handling missing values, correcting data types, detecting outliers, and creating summary visualizations

review.value: Exploratory Data Analysis: using visualizations and summary statistics to understand data structure, patterns, and anomalies before modeling
Creating new variables that better capture the information in raw data; often the difference between a mediocre and excellent model

learning_objectives: Build and interpret multiple linear regression models
Evaluate model fit using R-squared, adjusted R-squared, and RMSE
Diagnose and address common regression problems
readings: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Regression is the workhorse of business analytics. It answers the question: how do changes in input variables relate to changes in the outcome we care about?
key_concepts: Simple and multiple linear regression
Interpreting coefficients: holding other variables constant
Model evaluation: R-squared, adjusted R-squared, RMSE, p-values
Assumptions: linearity, independence, normality, equal variance
Multicollinearity, heteroscedasticity, and remedies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a regression model to predict house prices using multiple features, interpreting results, and diagnosing problems

review.value: Proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the model; ranges 0-1, but higher is not always better (overfitting risk)
When predictors are highly correlated with each other; inflates coefficient variance and makes individual predictor effects unreliable

learning_objectives: Build logistic regression models for binary classification
Evaluate classifiers using confusion matrix, accuracy, precision, recall, AUC
Apply decision trees and understand their business interpretability
readings: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Many business decisions are binary: will this customer churn? Is this transaction fraudulent? Will this loan default? Classification models turn data into actionable yes/no predictions.
key_concepts: Logistic regression: probability output between 0 and 1
Decision trees: intuitive, interpretable, but prone to overfitting
Confusion matrix: TP, FP, TN, FN
Precision vs. recall tradeoff and business implications
ROC curve and AUC for model comparison
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a customer churn prediction model: logistic regression and decision tree, comparing performance and business recommendations

review.value: Precision: of predicted positives, how many are correct. Recall: of actual positives, how many did we catch. Business context determines which matters more.
Area Under the ROC Curve: measures the model's ability to discriminate between classes across all thresholds; 0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect

learning_objectives: Design effective data visualizations following best practices
Build interactive business dashboards in Tableau or Power BI
Tell data stories that drive business action
readings: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics Ch. 4
Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (key principles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Analytics is only valuable if decision makers understand and act on the insights. Visualization translates complex analysis into clear, actionable communication.
key_concepts: Chart selection: matching visualization type to data type and message
Tufte's principles: data-ink ratio, avoid chartjunk, small multiples
Dashboard design: KPI selection, layout, interactivity, drill-down
Data storytelling: situation, complication, resolution framework
Color theory and accessibility in data visualization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an executive sales dashboard in Tableau: KPI cards, trend lines, geographic maps, and filter interactions
setup_requirements: Tableau Public or Power BI Desktop

review.value: Maximize the proportion of ink devoted to data; remove non-data elements (gridlines, backgrounds, decorations) that don't add information
Combining data, visuals, and narrative to communicate insights; starts with context, presents findings, and ends with clear recommendations

class_schedule.topic: Advanced Analytics: Clustering, Text Analytics, and AI Applications
learning_objectives: Apply K-means clustering for customer segmentation
Understand text analytics and sentiment analysis applications
Evaluate AI and machine learning opportunities in business
readings: Sharda et al., Business Intelligence and Analytics Ch. 8-9
McKinsey, The State of AI in Business (summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Advanced analytics techniques like clustering, text mining, and deep learning are becoming accessible to business users. Understanding when and how to apply them creates significant competitive advantage.
key_concepts: K-means clustering: unsupervised segmentation
Choosing optimal K: elbow method, silhouette score
Text analytics: tokenization, sentiment analysis, topic modeling
AI applications: recommendation engines, demand forecasting, chatbots
Responsible AI: bias detection, explainability, governance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Customer segmentation using K-means: clustering customers by behavior, profiling each segment, and designing targeted marketing strategies

review.value: Partitions data into K groups by minimizing within-cluster variance; useful for segmentation when groups are not predefined
Using NLP to classify text (reviews, social media) as positive, negative, or neutral; enables analyzing customer feedback at scale

questions.question: A regression model predicting quarterly sales has R-squared of 0.92 on training data but only 0.45 on new test data. Diagnose the problem and propose two remedies.
questions.answer: The model is overfitting: it has memorized patterns in the training data (including noise) rather than learning generalizable relationships, so it performs poorly on unseen data. Remedies: (1) Feature selection: remove predictors that add noise rather than signal; use stepwise selection, LASSO regularization, or VIF analysis to reduce model complexity. (2) Cross-validation: instead of a single train/test split, use k-fold cross-validation to get a more reliable estimate of model performance on new data. Additional remedies include increasing training data size or applying regularization (Ridge/LASSO) to penalize model complexity.

rubric.key: First remedy is specific and appropriate
Second remedy is specific and appropriate

questions.question: A dataset has 15% missing values in the 'income' column. Compare two approaches for handling this: deletion vs. mean imputation. When would each be appropriate?
questions.answer: Deletion (listwise): Removes all rows with missing income. Appropriate when missingness is completely random (MCAR) and the dataset is large enough that losing 15% of rows does not significantly reduce statistical power. Risk: if missingness is not random (e.g., high-income individuals refuse to report), deletion introduces bias. Mean imputation: Replaces missing values with the column mean. Appropriate when data is missing at random and you need to preserve sample size. Risk: it reduces variance in the income variable and can weaken correlations with other variables, potentially biasing results. Better alternatives exist: multiple imputation or model-based imputation that preserve the statistical properties of the data.

questions.question: A fraud detection model has 99% accuracy but only 20% recall for fraud cases. Explain why this is a problem and how you would improve the model for this business context.
questions.answer: High accuracy is misleading because fraud is rare (class imbalance). If 1% of transactions are fraudulent, predicting everything as 'not fraud' gives 99% accuracy but catches zero fraud. The 20% recall means the model misses 80% of actual fraud cases, which is unacceptable because the cost of missing fraud (chargebacks, customer trust) far exceeds the cost of false positives (investigating legitimate transactions). Improvements: (1) Adjust the classification threshold lower to catch more fraud at the cost of more false positives. (2) Use oversampling (SMOTE) or undersampling to address class imbalance. (3) Use a cost-sensitive learning approach that penalizes missed fraud more than false alarms. (4) Optimize for recall or F2-score rather than accuracy.

rubric.key: Identifies class imbalance as root cause
Explains business cost of low recall

questions.question: Your CEO asks for a dashboard showing 15 KPIs on a single screen. Explain why this is problematic and how you would design a better solution.
questions.answer: 15 KPIs on one screen creates cognitive overload: the viewer cannot focus on what matters, making the dashboard a wall of numbers rather than a decision-support tool. Better approach: (1) Prioritize: work with the CEO to identify 4-5 KPIs that are truly critical for their role (revenue, margin, customer growth, cash flow, top risk metric). (2) Design a summary dashboard with these top metrics prominently displayed with trend indicators and status colors. (3) Add drill-down capability: clicking any KPI reveals detailed sub-metrics and the remaining KPIs organized by theme. (4) Use exception-based alerting: secondary KPIs appear only when they deviate significantly from targets. This follows Tufte's principle of high data-ink ratio and Few's principle of dashboard simplicity.

major_assignments.title: End-to-End Business Analytics Project
major_assignments.description: Identify a real business problem, obtain relevant data, conduct exploratory analysis, build predictive models, create visualizations, and deliver actionable business recommendations. Present findings as a professional analytics report with an executive dashboard.
objectives: Apply the complete analytics lifecycle to a real business problem
Demonstrate proficiency in data preparation and analysis
Build and evaluate predictive models appropriate to the problem
Communicate findings through visualizations and clear narrative
deliverables: Analytics report (10-12 pages) with methodology and findings

criteria: Chart types appropriate for data and message
Well-defined business problem with appropriate analytical approach
Rigorous analysis with clean code and correct methodology
Actionable recommendations with business impact estimation

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of analytics concepts and techniques

hands_on_labs.description: Weekly lab exercises in Python/R, SQL, and visualization tools

midterm_examination.description: Covers data preparation, regression, and classification

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including visualization, clustering, and AI applications

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions require attendance. Analytical techniques build cumulatively; missing labs creates gaps.
course_policies.late_submissions: Lab submissions penalized 15% per day. Analytics project milestones have firm deadlines.
course_policies.collaboration: Analytics project may be individual or in pairs. All code must be written by team members (no copying from external sources without citation).
course_policies.academic_integrity: Code must be original or properly attributed. Using pre-built models without understanding and modifying them is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Python (Anaconda) or R. Tableau Public or Power BI Desktop. SQL client for database exercises.
